coredata.dc:title: Hemoglobin-Capped carbon dots synthesized via microwave green approach as a biosensor for specific cholesterol detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Shahabadi, Nahid
Omidfar, Kobra
Zendehcheshm, Saba
coredata.dc:description: This study explores the potential of hemoglobin-capped green-synthesized carbon dots (g-CD) as a biosensor for the specific detection of cholesterol. The CDs, synthesized using an environmentally friendly and convenient microwave green approach, derived from Wood Extract of Wild Cherry Shrub and utilizing hemoglobin as a capping agent, offer promising characteristics for cholesterol detection. This green synthesis method avoids the use of toxic precursors, making the process safer and more sustainable. Additionally, microwave (MW) heating, being volumetric in nature, reduces the synthesis time and energy consumption, resulting in a uniform and unique microstructure, offering a substantial advantage over traditional methods. Through fluorescence spectroscopy, the interaction between the g-CD/Hb complex and cholesterol was investigated. The results demonstrate that the fluorescence intensity of the g-CD/Hb complex increases with the concentration of cholesterol, suggesting a potential application for cholesterol detection. Notably, the calculated limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 9.22 μM, significantly lower than previously reported sensors, indicating higher sensitivity. Moreover, the biosensor exhibits high selectivity towards cholesterol over other biomolecules commonly found in human serum. Additionally, the biosensor shows excellent performance with real human blood samples, indicating its practical utility in clinical settings. Overall, the g-CD/Hb biosensor presents a promising approach for the specific detection of cholesterol, offering potential applications in biomedical research and clinical diagnostics. The key innovations include the use of a green and rapid synthesis method, improved detection limit, and validation of performance in real samples, distinguishing this sensor from previous research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green carbon dots
Biosensor
Hemoglobin
Cholesterol
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence off/on of the (g-CD)/Hb complex solution in the presence of cholesterol
Detection of cholesterol utilizing the (g-CD)/Hb complex

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors express their gratitude to the Razi University Research Council for their support in this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The dried branches and (B) Filtering of cerasus microcarpa (wild Cherry) shrub' extract.

(A) UV–Vis absorption and photographs of g-CD in aqueous solution under white light (left) and UV light at 395 nm (right), (B) The corresponding excitation and emission peaks of g-CD, (C) FT-IR spectrum of g-CD and Extract, (D) TEM image and Histogram of g-CD.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of g-CD/Hb complex excited at 400 nm. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of g-CD, Hb, and emission spectrum of g-CD excited at 400 nm. (C) Fluorescence spectra of (g-CD/Hb) complex at different concentration of Cho (0–110.8 μM) and (D) plot of log FCho/F(g-CD)/Hb as a function of Ccho.

Fluorescence spectra of (A) g-CD/Hb/cholesterol, (B) g-CD/cholesterol excited at 400 nm, (C) Time course of PL intensity of aqueous g-CD solution (0.001 mg/mL) after add Hb, (D) Plot of log (FBio/F(g-CD)/Hb) for different biomaterials at the final concentration of 110.8 μM.
The accuracy of the determined cholesterol concentrations in human blood serum derived from both endogenous and exogenous sources.

head.ce:title: Hemoglobin-Capped carbon dots synthesized via microwave green approach as a biosensor for specific cholesterol detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Inorganic Chemistry Department, Situated in the Faculty of Chemistry at Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Situated in the Faculty of Chemistry at Razi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Inorganic Chemistry Department, Situated in the Faculty of Chemistry at Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biosensor Research Center, Operating within the Endocrinology and Metabolism Molecular-Cellular Sciences Institute at Tehran University of Medical Science, Tehran P.O. Box:1411713137, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Operating within the Endocrinology and Metabolism Molecular-Cellular Sciences Institute at

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Biosensor Research Center, Operating within the Endocrinology and Metabolism Molecular-Cellular Sciences Institute at Tehran University of Medical Science, Tehran P.O. Box:1411713137, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Endocrinology and Metabolism Research Center, Part of the Endocrinology and Metabolism Research Institute at Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Part of the Endocrinology and Metabolism Research Institute at

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Endocrinology and Metabolism Research Center, Part of the Endocrinology and Metabolism Research Institute at Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study explores the potential of hemoglobin-capped green-synthesized carbon dots (g-CD) as a biosensor for the specific detection of cholesterol. The CDs, synthesized using an environmentally friendly and convenient microwave green approach, derived from Wood Extract of Wild Cherry Shrub and utilizing hemoglobin as a capping agent, offer promising characteristics for cholesterol detection. This green synthesis method avoids the use of toxic precursors, making the process safer and more sustainable. Additionally, microwave (MW) heating, being volumetric in nature, reduces the synthesis time and energy consumption, resulting in a uniform and unique microstructure, offering a substantial advantage over traditional methods. Through fluorescence spectroscopy, the interaction between the g-CD/Hb complex and cholesterol was investigated. The results demonstrate that the fluorescence intensity of the g-CD/Hb complex increases with the concentration of cholesterol, suggesting a potential application for cholesterol detection. Notably, the calculated limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 9.22 μM, significantly lower than previously reported sensors, indicating higher sensitivity. Moreover, the biosensor exhibits high selectivity towards cholesterol over other biomolecules commonly found in human serum. Additionally, the biosensor shows excellent performance with real human blood samples, indicating its practical utility in clinical settings. Overall, the g-CD/Hb biosensor presents a promising approach for the specific detection of cholesterol, offering potential applications in biomedical research and clinical diagnostics. The key innovations include the use of a green and rapid synthesis method, improved detection limit, and validation of performance in real samples, distinguishing this sensor from previous research.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cholesterol is crucial for building cell membranes, supporting regular cellular functions, and developing cell structures during the entire human lifespan. It primarily comes from dietary sources and is extensively found in both human and animal bodies, especially in organs such as the liver, spleen, kidneys, and skin, and is notably abundant in brain tissue [1]. The typical cholesterol concentration in the human body ranges from 2.7 to 5.4 mM [2]. Maintaining cholesterol levels within this range is essential for overall health. Low concentrations of cholesterol have been associated with adverse effects, such as anemia, reduced human resistance, and the potential development of conditions like hyperthyroidism, anemia, depression, and decreased immunity [2]. Conversely, elevated levels of cholesterol can result in blood vessel damage and an increased susceptibility to coronary heart disease [3]. Consequently, maintaining cholesterol within an optimal range is crucial for human health. The detection of cholesterol serves as a critical biomarker for cardiovascular disease and hypertension, playing a pivotal role in clinical diagnosis and health management. Various methods are employed for cholesterol detection, including chromatography [4], electrochemical methods [5], enzyme colorimetric methods [6], and spectrophotometric techniques [7]. However, existing methods often suffer from drawbacks such as prolonged reaction times, complex detection processes, and limited sensitivity.

Recently, advanced techniques such as Molecular Imprinting and Surface-Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy (SERS) have emerged as innovative approaches to address these challenges. Chen et al. explored a SERS detection strategy based on molecular imprinting for the quantification of large proteins and the reduction of non-specific recognition, demonstrating remarkable advancements in this area [8]. Additionally, Arabi et al. proposed green and environmentally friendly methods for molecular imprinting, which not only offer high efficiency but also emphasize sustainability, making them highly valuable for advanced applications [9,10]. Furthermore, Arabi and Ostovan utilized label-free SERS strategies for the detection of Raman-inactive protein biomarkers, aiming to achieve ultra-sensitivity and broad applicability [11]. The chiral molecular imprinting-based SERS strategy for absolute enantiomeric discrimination is another significant advancement in this field [12].

Mostly, methods utilizing cholesterol oxidase enzymes are employed for cholesterol estimation due to their specificity and rapid analysis potential. However, the implementation of these methods necessitates the maintenance of sterile and stringent environmental conditions, making them costly. Additionally, the crucial task of enzyme fabrication onto a suitable matrix while retaining its properties further complicates the process. Research is ongoing to explore new matrices that prevent the bioleaching of enzymes. The effectiveness of a biosensor, encompassing selectivity, sensitivity, and stability, hinges on the judicious selection of a matrix for immobilization [13]. Metal nanoparticles and quantum dots have been acknowledged for their heightened electrocatalytic activity and notable stability [13]. Carbon dots (CDs) are gaining preference over semiconductor quantum dots in various fields due to their advantageous properties, including biocompatibility, chemical inertness, non-toxicity, low cost, and facile synthesis procedures [14]. With these distinctive properties, carbon dots find applications in diverse fields, including chemical sensing [15,16], bioimaging [17], photocatalysis [18], dye degradation [19], and energy storage [20]. Various techniques are utilized for CD synthesis, categorized broadly into 'top-down' and 'bottom-up' approaches. In top-down methods, carbon dots are derived from large-scale carbon sources, whereas in bottom-up synthesis, they are synthesized from smaller carbon materials [21]. Nevertheless, the preparation of CDs often involves the use of chemical agents such as potent oxidizers or acids, posing potential risks to human health and environmental pollution. Lately, there has been a growing interest in biogenic carbon dots, primarily attributed to their advantages over carbon dots derived from hazardous chemicals. Bio-precursor-derived carbon dots offer advantages such as non-toxicity, bio-accessibility, and cost-efficiency. Notably, their environmentally friendly characteristics, making a significant contribution to their acceptance, are of paramount importance [22,23]. For the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), natural sources like tejpata leaves, orange juice, and amino acids have been employed [24].

Hemoglobin (Hb), a metalloprotein found in erythrocytes, comprises globin (the protein component) and heme (a conjugated structure housing an iron(II) atom at the core and four porphyrin N atoms arranged at the vertices of the face) [25]. Hb plays a pivotal role in transporting oxygen from the lungs to various bodily tissues and in facilitating the elimination of carbon dioxide from tissues to the lungs. The conjugated structure of the heme group in Hb allows for its interaction with carbon dots (CDs) through π–π interactions, resulting in the quenching of CD fluorescence. In addition to the four heme groups, Hb consists of four polypeptide chains capable of non-specifically absorbing compounds with hydrophobic moieties (e.g., cholesterol) through hydrophobic interactions. Hydrophobic interaction is thermodynamically more stable than π–π interaction [26]. Consequently, these interactions are suitable for use in biosensing applications. A hydrophobic analyte can be identified through its specific interaction with Hb, which interrupts the existing π-π interaction between Hb and CD, thereby increasing the fluorescence of CD. Considering the hydrophobic characteristics of cholesterol, we propose the creation of an innovative cholesterol biosensor that employs the CD/Hb complex and utilizes competitive interactions. In our research, we have successfully created highly fluorescent carbon dots using an environmentally friendly microwave technique, originating from the Wood Extract of Wild Cherry Shrub. We observed fluorescence quenching upon forming a complex between Hb and g-CD. Introducing cholesterol into the (g-CD)/Hb complex solution in water resulted in enhanced fluorescence. This biosensor, based on the (g-CD)/Hb complex, stands out due to its simplicity, high sensitivity and selectivity, speed, environmental sustainability, and cost efficiency in cholesterol detection. Therefore, the (g-CD)/Hb complex demonstrates considerable promise as an effective tool for monitoring blood cholesterol levels.

The branches of the Cerasus microcarpa (wild cherry) shrub, as shown in Fig. 1 A, were collected from Shaho Mountain in Kermanshah province, Iran. They were then air-dried in the shade and subsequently crushed into small pieces. Glucose, galactose, urea, cholesterol, L-ascorbic acid, Triton X-100, a dialysis tube with a typical molecular weight cut-off of 10,000 Da, lysozyme chloride derived from chicken egg white with a purity greater than 90 %, human hemoglobin with a purity greater than 98 %, and metal salts including sodium chloride, iron(II) chloride tetrahydrate, iron(III) chloride hexahydrate, magnesium nitrate hexahydrate, and copper(II) nitrate trihydrate were procured from Sigma Aldrich and Merck Millipore for experimental purposes. Deionized (DI) water was utilized in all experiments, and analytical-grade reagents were employed without the need for further purification.

The fluorescence spectra were obtained using a JASCO FP 6200 spectrometer equipped with a quartz cuvette, employing a slit range of 1 nm. UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded using an HP spectrophotometer (Agilent 8453) with a quartz cuvette of 1 cm path length. Fourier Transform Infra-Red Spectra (FT-IR) was recorded using a Bruker ALPHA FT-IR spectrometer, which scans wavelengths from 4000 to 400 cm−1. For Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), a Zeiss-EM10C-100 KV model was utilized. Samples were deposited onto carbon-coated TEM copper grids and allowed to air-dry before imaging. The average particle size was calculated using the Image J software.

Green carbon dots were produced through a simple microwave-assisted method in a single step. Firstly, 40 g of branches from the Cerasus microcarpa (wild cherry) shrub was boiled in 400 mL of double distilled water for 1 h under stirring with a magnetic stirrer. Subsequently, the color of the sample turned red, as shown in Fig. 1B. The resulting solution was then filtered (Fig. 1B) and subjected to microwave irradiation in a domestic microwave oven for 10 min at 900 W. The resulting brownish-dry residue was subsequently stirred magnetically with distilled water and centrifuged for 15 min at 6000 rpm to eliminate large particles. Additionally, the resulting solution underwent dialysis using a 10 kDa MWCO dialysis membrane against distilled water for 48 h. The resultant yellowish-brown solution was then stored at 4 °C for further utilization.

Initially, we determined the concentration of the g-CD stock solution by drying 5 cc of g-CD at 75 °C in an oven. Subsequently, to facilitate interaction with hemoglobin, we diluted the stock solution of carbon dots with distilled water. The final concentration of (g-CD) used for the interaction was 0.001 mg/ml. We investigated its interaction with different concentrations of hemoglobin ranging from 4.65 to 12.77 µM. The fluorescence of the aqueous solutions containing (g-CD) and hemoglobin was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, with an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. Additionally, we studied the complexation of g-CD with various materials, including lysozyme, L-ascorbic acid, galactose, glucose, urea, copper(II), sodium chloride, iron(II), iron(III), and magnesium(II), all at a consistent final concentration of 8.89 µM. The fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) was calculated using the overlap between the fluorescence emission spectrum of g-CD and the UV absorption spectrum of hemoglobin. Both the emission spectrum of g-CD and the UV absorption spectrum of hemoglobin were recorded within the range of 410–600 nm at the same concentration (0.001 mg/mL).

To establish the linear detection range for cholesterol, we initially prepared a solution with a concentration of 0.1 mg/ml. This was achieved by dissolving 10 mg of cholesterol powder in 1.5 ml of ethanol, followed by the addition of 1 ml of Triton X-100. The resulting mixture was agitated for 5 min at a speed of 400 rpm to ensure uniformity.

Next, to create a stock solution with a concentration of 1 mg/ml, 7.5 ml of distilled water was gradually added to the previous mixture while it was being rotated. Subsequently, a cholesterol stock solution with a concentration of 0.1 mg/ml was obtained by diluting the above solution with distilled water.

In the standard assay procedure, the g-CD (0.001 mg/mL)/Hb (8.89 μM) complex was prepared using stock solutions of g-CD (0.1 mg/mL) and Hb (1.00 × 10−4 M). Then, varying amounts of cholesterol stock solutions (0.1 mg/mL) were added to the (g-CD)/Hb complex to determine the linear range of cholesterol detection. For human serum measurements, plasma samples with varying cholesterol concentrations were prepared from Bahar Laboratory (Fig. S1). For assays involving human serum, blood plasma was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm (10 g) for 20 min to remove large molecules, and then dissolved in ethanol (99 %) to make a stock solution. The cholesterol level in human blood plasma was independently determined through a (CHOD/POD method). To quantify the cholesterol content, the samples underwent dilution with distilled water. Then different amounts of prepared cholesterol solution were added to real plasma samples. Subsequently, a predetermined volume of the diluted samples after spiking was introduced into the (g-CD)/Hb mixture, and the resulting fluorescence emission was analyzed using an excitation wavelength of 400 nm and an emission wavelength of 475 nm.

Cholesterol is a critical biomolecule in the human body, playing a central role in cellular structure, hormone synthesis, and the production of bile acids necessary for digestion. However, elevated levels of cholesterol, particularly low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol, are strongly associated with the development of cardiovascular diseases (CVDs), including atherosclerosis, heart attacks, and strokes. Given the widespread prevalence and significant health impact of CVDs, cholesterol monitoring has become essential for early diagnosis, risk assessment, and management of these conditions. Sensors designed to detect cholesterol levels offer a convenient, rapid, and non-invasive method to monitor this biomarker, providing vital information for both preventive and therapeutic healthcare. In addition to its importance in health monitoring, cholesterol is also a suitable analyte for sensor development due to its well-understood biochemical properties and the availability of specific enzymes, like cholesterol oxidase and cholesterol esterase, that can be used to create selective and sensitive detection mechanisms. Cholesterol sensors typically employ these enzymes to catalyze reactions that produce measurable signals, such as changes in current or fluorescence, which correlate with cholesterol concentration. The ability to engineer sensors with high specificity, coupled with the clinical relevance of cholesterol measurement, makes it an ideal candidate for analyte detection in both laboratory settings and point-of-care applications [27,28].

Opting for green-synthesized carbon dots for sensing is a strategic choice due to their environmentally friendly and sustainable nature. Green synthesis methods typically utilize natural precursors like plants, fruits, or biomass, which significantly reduce the environmental impact compared to traditional chemical synthesis. This approach avoids the use of toxic solvents and harsh chemicals, making the process safer and more cost-effective. Additionally, green-synthesized carbon dots often possess unique functional groups derived from their natural sources, which can enhance their interaction with biological molecules such as cholesterol. This enhanced interaction is crucial for developing sensitive and selective sensors, as it improves the detection capability of the sensor while maintaining biocompatibility, which is essential for biomedical applications. Furthermore, green-synthesized carbon dots exhibit excellent optical properties, such as strong fluorescence, which are highly beneficial for sensing applications. These properties enable the detection of analyte at low concentrations, making the sensors both sensitive and reliable. The biocompatibility of these carbon dots, derived from their natural origins, ensures that they can be safely used in living systems without causing harm. Overall, the combination of environmental sustainability, superior optical characteristics, and high biocompatibility makes green-synthesized carbon dots a preferred choice for the development of advanced sensors [29,30].

Hemoglobin is chosen for cholesterol sensors primarily due to its excellent redox properties and biocompatibility. The iron-containing heme groups in hemoglobin enable it to participate in electron transfer reactions, which are crucial for the electrochemical detection of cholesterol. This ability to facilitate redox reactions makes hemoglobin an effective mediator in biosensors, allowing for the conversion of cholesterol binding events into measurable electrical signals. Additionally, being a naturally occurring protein in the human body, hemoglobin is biocompatible, reducing the risk of adverse reactions when used in medical or biological applications. Its stability under various environmental conditions further enhances its suitability for long-term use in cholesterol sensing. Moreover, hemoglobin's surface chemistry, with multiple functional groups, allows it to be easily modified or conjugated with other molecules, such as cholesterol-specific antibodies or enzymes. This versatility enables the design of more complex and highly sensitive sensor systems. Hemoglobin can also act as a catalyst, enhancing the reaction rates necessary for detecting cholesterol. Its high affinity for oxygen and other small molecules can be leveraged to monitor cholesterol levels, making it a multifaceted component in biosensor design. These combined properties make hemoglobin an excellent choice for creating effective, reliable, and durable cholesterol sensors [31,32].

Green carbon dots (g-CD) were synthesized through the microwave heating of the Wood Extract of Wild Cherry Shrub. Despite continuous research endeavors, the exact mechanism underlying the formation of carbon dots from organic compounds remains largely unclear. Microwave irradiation is utilized as a technique for the bottom-up synthesis of carbon nanomaterials from the constituent small molecules within the extract. This process entails several consecutive steps, including condensation, polymerization, and carbonization, culminating in the generation of fluorescent carbon dots via nucleation burst. The acidic groups present in the precursor material play a crucial role in facilitating this formation process [33].

The UV–visible absorption spectrum of g-CD has been shown in Fig. 2 A. Where the major absorption band was noticed at 272 nm corresponding to π-π* transition of sp2 CC bond [34]. Microwave irradiation of the Wood Extract of Wild Cherry Shrub resulted in a yellowish brown solution that displayed green fluorescence under UV irradiation (Fig. 2A). Confirmation of CD formation via the Microwave method was attained through the fluorescence emitted upon exposure to a UV light source [35].

The fluorescence spectrum of the g-CD is depicted in Fig. 2B, revealing emission peaks at 475 nm when excited at 400 nm. This system displays excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, examined by recording fluorescence spectra of the g-CD across various excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 420 nm. Discrepancies observed in intensity and emission peaks of the g-CD under different excitation wavelengths may stem from the polydispersity and surface functionalities of the carbon dots [36]. The investigation into excitation dependence indicates that the system exhibits maximal intensity when excited at 400 nm, with emission spectra centered at 475 nm. These specific excitation and emission wavelengths were selected for the g-CD. The corresponding excitation and emission peaks are illustrated in Fig. 2B. The fluorescence stability of CDs is crucial for their practical use. As illustrated in Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensity of the g-CD solution remained high even after 120 days at 4 °C, indicating long-term stability. Additionally, the carbon dot solution remained clear and transparent during this time.

To examine the components, surface groups, and structure of the green synthesized carbon dots, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) analysis was conducted. Fig. 2C displays the FT-IR spectra of both the Extract (in red) and the green synthesized carbon dots (in green). The Extract exhibits various functional groups, with the broadest peak comprising two distinct peaks. One peak corresponds to the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group O-H at 3456 cm−1, while the other peak at 3387 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of the NH group. Additionally, a peak is observed at 2074 cm−1, indicating the presence of CC bonds, and another peak at 1637 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups CO [14]. The band at 1119 cm−1 is attributed to the CO stretching vibrations of both carboxylic and alcoholic functionalities [37]. Furthermore, the peaks observed at 626 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1 are likely attributed to the C-H bending out-of-plane deformation and symmetric stretching vibration of COO−, respectively [38]. The spectrum of green-synthesized carbon dots (g-CD) displayed bands at 3452, 2077, 1637, 1385, 1105, and 645 cm−1, which can be associated with the stretching vibrations of OH, NH, CC, CO, COO−, CO, and CH bending, respectively. The FT-IR analysis collectively demonstrates the existence of varied hydrophilic functionalities, including carboxylic, amino, and hydroxyl groups, on the surface of the g-CDs. These functional groups play a significant role in improving the water solubility and stability of the CDs. Consequently, the augmented solubility and stability of CDs in aqueous media substantially expand their potential applications as sensors for water samples [39].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, along with the accompanying histogram, were employed to evaluate the morphology, distribution, and particle size of the carbon dots (CDs) [39]. Examination of Fig. 2D reveals the near-spherical nature of the g-CD. The average size of the prepared g-CD was approximately 4.08 nm, with a particle size distribution ranging from 1.62 nm to 8.16 nm.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) is a valuable structural technique widely used to analyze carbon dots (CDs). It offers insights into particle size, phase purity, and the crystalline properties of CDs. XRD can also determine the crystal spacing within the crystalline carbon cores of CDs and measure their unit cell dimensions [40,41]. The XRD pattern of g-CD typically shows a broad peak around 2θ = 21.50°, indicating the amorphous nature of g-CD, as seen in Fig. S3 [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence off/on of the (g-CD)/Hb complex solution in the presence of cholesterol
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated the interaction between green carbon dots (g-CD) and hemoglobin (Hb) using fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3 A, when Hb was added to a solution of g-CD, the fluorescence of g-CD significantly decreased. Specifically, the fluorescence intensity of g-CD was halved upon the formation of the (g-CD)-Hb complex, suggesting potential interactions facilitated by van der Waals forces between the aromatic ring system of g-CD and the heme group on Hb.

In such interactions, the excited state energy level of the donor molecule must exceed the ground state energy level of the acceptor molecule to drive the transfer of an electron from the donor to the acceptor. This energy requirement is crucial for a process known as photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [42]. UV–visible absorption spectra of g-CD and Hb (refer to Fig. 3B) revealed distinct energy absorption characteristics.

Notably, the energy absorption of g-CD, with an absorbance peak at 272 nm corresponding to 4.56 electron volts (eV), exceeded that of Hb, which showed an absorbance peak at 404 nm corresponding to 3.07 eV. These results suggest a fluorescence quenching mechanism mediated by the PET process, where the heme group of Hb acts as the acceptor and the aromatic ring system of g-CD serves as the donor. A similar fluorescence quenching mechanism involving the PET process has been documented in a CD/metal ion complex [43]. To explore whether quenching could arise from π-π interactions between the heme groups of Hb and the aromatic rings of carbon dots (g-CD), we introduced lysozyme protein (which lacks heme groups unlike Hb) into an aqueous g-CD solution. This achieved a final concentration equivalent to that of Hb. As depicted in Fig. S4, logarithmic relative photoluminescence (PL) intensity values were computed. The Log (F(g-CD)/F) values of Hb and lysozyme were 0.344 and 0.022, respectively, indicating a correlation between the extent of fluorescence quenching and the number of heme groups per molecule. We also evaluated the fluorescence quenching of metal ions, including Cu(II), Fe(II), Fe(III), Mg(II), and NaCl, at the same final concentration (8.89 μM) [43,44]. The log (F(g-CD)/F) values of these metal ions (0.031 for Cu(II), −0.002 for Fe(II), 0.020 for Fe(III), −0.007 for NaCl, and −0.005 for Mg(II) ions) were notably lower than that of Hb (0.344). This difference is due to the lower electron densities of metal ions compared to the heme group of Hb. Additionally, we examined common biomolecules such as L-ascorbic acid, galactose, glucose, and urea. Their log (F(g-CD)/F) values were also notably lower than that of Hb (0.016 for L-ascorbic acid, −0.008 for galactose, −0.009 for glucose, and −0.010 for urea), indicating the absence of fluorescence quenching. Thus, Hb forms a complex with g-CD and quenches g-CD fluorescence through interactions involving its heme group.

The overlap between the absorption spectrum of hemoglobin and the emission spectrum of g-CD, as shown in Fig. S5, leads to resonance energy transfer. This process is based on Förster's theory of non-radiative energy transfer [45], which suggests that when the fluorescence emitted is absorbed by a molecule during interaction, energy transfer can occur between the two entities. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) was utilized to measure the distance between g-CD and hemoglobin during their interaction. As illustrated in Fig. 25, there is a significant overlap between the emission spectrum of g-CD and the absorption spectrum of hemoglobin. To analyze this energy transfer, the efficiency of the energy transfer (E) must first be determined, which is essential for calculating the overlap integral, the critical distance (Förster distance), and the binding distance. The following equation (Eq. (1)) is used to calculate the energy transfer efficiency.
(1)
E = 1 - F /
F 0
=
R 0 6
/
(
R 0 6
+
r 6
)

In this equation, r represents the distance between g-CD and hemoglobin, while R0 is the Förster distance, which corresponds to the distance at which the energy transfer efficiency is 50 %. The Förster distance R0 was calculated using the following equation:
(2)
R 0 6
= 8.79 ×
10
- 25
k 2
n
- 4
φ J

In Eq. (2), k^2 is the spatial orientation factor (with a value of 2/3), n is the refractive index of the medium (1.33), and φ represents the fluorescence quantum yield of g-CD (0.146). The parameter J is the overlap integral between the absorption spectrum of hemoglobin and the fluorescence emission spectrum of g-CD, calculated using the following equation:
(3)
J = ∫
F
λ
ε
λ
λ 4
d λ
/ ∫
F
λ
d λ

In this equation, ∊(λ) is the molar absorption coefficient of hemoglobin at wavelength λ, and F(λ) is the fluorescence intensity of g-CD at wavelength λ [46]. In our study, we determined that J=3.35 × 10−14 cm3 L/mol, R0=3.11 nm, E=0.53, and r = 3.05 nm, using the above equations. These results suggest that hemoglobin is in close proximity to g-CD (with r = 3.05 nm), indicating a strong interaction between them. Additionally, since the r value is less than 8 nm, it can be inferred that the mechanism of this interaction is likely static [47].

Cholesterol displays amphiphilic characteristics, featuring a hydrophilic hydroxyl group and hydrophobic alkyl chains. These hydrophobic alkyl chains tend to interact nonspecifically with the hydrophobic patches of Hb via hydrophobic interactions. Consequently, the association of cholesterol with Hb has the potential to disrupt the (g-CD)/Hb complex, leading to the re-establishment of g-CD fluorescence. Figs. 3C and 4A illustrate the fluorescence intensity of the (g-CD)/Hb complex (at concentrations of 0.001 mg/mL and 8.89 μM, respectively) after the addition of cholesterol (0–110.8 μM). The observed 70 % increase in fluorescence suggests that the Hb/cholesterol complex is thermodynamically more favorable compared to the (g-CD)/Hb complex. Hydrophobic interactions exhibit an energy level of approximately 40 kJ/mol, surpassing that of π-π interactions (van der Waals forces) by a factor of 10 (ranging from 0.4 to 4.0 kJ/mol) [48]. For control purposes, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity of g-CD remained unaltered upon the addition of cholesterol (Fig. 4 B), suggesting that cholesterol does not exert an influence on the fluorescence intensity of g-CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of cholesterol utilizing the (g-CD)/Hb complex
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the (g-CD)/Hb complex for biosensor applications, we mixed different concentrations of Hb and g-CD in deionized water to create various final concentrations. In Fig. 3A, we observed that the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of g-CD decreased gradually as the concentration of Hb increased under excitation at 400 nm. The degree of quenching increased rapidly with higher Hb concentrations initially, then reached a plateau with further increases in [Hb]. At [Hb] = 8.89 and 12.77 μM, approximately 55 % and 72 % of the fluorescence intensity of g-CD was quenched, respectively. Consequently, unless specified otherwise, we used final concentrations of 8.89 μM for Hb and 0.001 mg/mL for g-CD in subsequent experiments involving the (g-CD)/Hb complex. We also investigated the time required to quench the fluorescence intensity of g-CD. In Fig. 4C, we observed the change in F(g-CD)/Hb over time at [Hb] = 4.65 and 8.89 μM. Quenching of g-CD fluorescence reached saturation within 60 s after the addition of Hb at both tested concentrations, indicating the rapid formation of the (g-CD)/Hb complex within one minute.

The study investigated the range within which cholesterol levels could be detected using the (g-CD)/Hb complex. Fig. 3C shows fluorescence spectra obtained when exciting the samples at 400 nm. These spectra depict the fluorescence emitted by solutions containing different concentrations of cholesterol along with the (g-CD)/Hb complex. Additionally, a graph plotting the ratio of fluorescence intensity before and after adding cholesterol (log FCho/F(g-CD)/Hb) against the concentration of cholesterol (Ccholesterol) is presented. As the concentration of cholesterol (Ccholesterol) increased, there was a gradual increase in the fluorescence intensity of the (g-CD)/Hb complex. The log FCho/F(g-CD)/Hb value demonstrated a linear increase with the rise in Ccho, following the Eq. (4) (r2 = 0.991) (Fig. 3D).
(4)
Log
F
Cho
/
F
g - C D
/ H b
=
4.4728
Cho
+ 0.0336

In the investigated concentration range of 0–110.8 μM (0–3.75 mg/dL) for Ccholesterol, the limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 9.22 μM [49]. According to our findings, the linear range aligns with previously reported cholesterol sensors, while the LOD surpasses those of existing sensors [26,50–53] (Table S1). This linear range is deemed suitable for medical applications [54].

The study examined how well the (g-CD)/Hb complex could distinguish cholesterol from other common biomolecules found in human serum, such as L-ascorbic acid, galactose, urea, and glucose. Fig. 4D shows the comparison of fluorescence intensity ratios (log (FBio/F(g-CD/Hb)) when these biomolecules were introduced at the same final concentration of 110.8 μM. Only cholesterol caused an increase in fluorescence, while the other biomolecules led to a decrease. This occurred because these hydrophilic biomolecules hinder interactions with Hb in water, unlike cholesterol.

The unique interactions among carbon dots (CD), hemoglobin (Hb), and cholesterol allow for the development of an optical cholesterol sensor with improved sensitivity and selectivity. Considering the significance of rapid detection and pH conditions in biosensor design, the performance of the (g-CD)/Hb complex was evaluated under these parameters. Fig. S6A demonstrates the fluorescence intensity of (g-CD)/Hb complex solutions over time after adding cholesterol at a concentration of 86.20 μM. The fluorescence reached its maximum level within 2 min, suggesting its suitability for biosensor applications. Fig. S6B displays the relationship between the log (FCho/F(g-CD/Hb)) ratio and pH. This ratio remained consistent across the pH range from 6.2 to 7.6, indicating that this biosensor can effectively operate with human serum under physiological pH conditions.

The study evaluated the effectiveness of the g-CD/Hb complex in detecting cholesterol in human plasma serum. Initially, the cholesterol levels in human serum were measured using a (CHOD/POD method). Then, specific amounts of cholesterol were added to the serum after removing the plasma through centrifugation. The total cholesterol concentration, comprising both the original and added amounts, was calculated by analyzing the fluorescence spectra using a calibration curve (refer to Fig. 3D). Table 1 provides a comparison between the measured and known concentrations of cholesterol in human serum. Cholesterol recovery rates for five samples were computed using Eq. (5).
(5)
Recovery
%
=
(
( Measuredcholesterol - Originalcholesterol )
/ Addedcholesterol )
× 100 %

The results suggest the appropriateness of employing this cholesterol biosensor with authentic human blood samples. Despite slight discrepancies from 100 %, potentially attributed to interference from other biomaterials present in human serum, these variations were insignificant.

The hemoglobin-capped green-synthesized carbon dot biosensor offers several advantages, including ease of synthesis, biocompatibility, and specificity for cholesterol detection. These findings underscore the potential of this biosensing platform for various biomedical applications, including disease diagnostics and monitoring. Future research efforts can focus on further optimizing the biosensor's sensitivity, selectivity, and stability to enhance its performance in clinical and research settings. Overall, the hemoglobin-capped green-synthesized carbon dot biosensor represents a promising advancement in the field of cholesterol detection, with potential implications for improving healthcare outcomes and biomedical research.
Nahid Shahabadi: Conceptualization. Kobra Omidfar: Data curation. Saba Zendehcheshm: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their gratitude to the Razi University Research Council for their support in this study.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111652.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
